concept.title: Anatomy of a validation rule
concept.shortdesc: Every markdown-validator rule is a JSON object with nine fields. Understanding what each field does is the foundation for writing rules that express your editorial standards accurately.

othermeta.name: description
othermeta.content: Understand the purpose of every field in a markdown-validator rule: id, name, type, query, flag, operation, value, level, and mitigation.

section.title: The nine rule fields
section.codeblock: { "id": 1, "name": "topic-must-be-tutorial", "type": "header", "query": "ms.topic", "flag": "value", "operation": "==", "value": "tutorial", "level": "Required", "mitigation":"Set ms.topic: tutorial in the front matter." }

dlentry.dd: A unique integer that identifies this rule. IDs are used in workflow definitions to refer to rules. Assign IDs sequentially within a rule set and do not reuse them.

A human-readable label shown in validation reports. Use lowercase words separated by hyphens (for example, description-must-exist). Choose a name that reads like an assertion so the failure is immediately clear.

Either "header" or "body". Header rules check YAML front-matter; body rules check the rendered HTML. You can omit this field if the rule is in the correct section of the rule set file — it is injected automatically.

dd.p: What to look up in the document.

ul.li: For header rules: the metadata key name (for example, "ms.topic").
For body rules: an XPath expression against the rendered HTML (for example, "/html/body/h1").

dlentry.dd: How to process the query result before comparing it with value. For header rules, common flags are value, check, date, and pattern. For body rules, common flags are count, text, and dom. See Flag reference for the complete list.

The comparison operator applied between the processed query result and value. Examples: "==" (equals), "!=" (not equals), ">" (greater than), "[]" (contains). See the Operator reference for the complete list.

The expected value the query result is compared against. Always a string. For numeric comparisons the string is coerced to a number at evaluation time. For multi-value checks (with "[]"), use a comma-separated list: "guide, article, topic".
Severity of a failure: "Required" (blocks the CI pipeline) or "Suggested" (informational, does not block the pipeline). Defaults to "Required" if omitted.

A plain-English message shown to authors when this rule fails. Write the mitigation as a direct instruction: what the author should do to fix the problem. This message is the primary communication between your rule and your authors — make it clear and actionable.

section.title: How evaluation works
section.p: The tool resolves query, processes the result with flag, applies operation against value, and returns pass or fail. If the query returns nothing (key absent, no XPath matches), the rule fails with the mitigation message.
